    Radiatively Inefficient Accretion Flows

    RIAF / ADAF Models

    Type: Accretion flow theory
    Also known as: ADAF, hot accretion flow
    Key property: L << LEdd; ions and electrons thermally decoupled
    Key papers: Narayan & Yi (1994, 1995),
    Yuan & Narayan (2014)
    Related: Bondi Accretion
    Faraday Rotation
    3C 84 / NGC 1275 Jet
    AGN Feedback

    Radiatively inefficient accretion flows (RIAFs), also termed advection-dominated accretion flows (ADAFs), are hot, geometrically thick, optically thin accretion solutions that arise when the mass accretion rate is well below the Eddington rate [1][2]. In this regime, viscous heating of the gas cannot be efficiently radiated away; instead, the thermal energy is advected inward with the flow or carried outward in winds and jets [1][3]. RIAFs are the standard accretion model for low-luminosity AGN, including the supermassive black holes in M87 and 3C 84 (NGC 1275) [3][4].

    Hot Accretion Flow Theory

    Narayan & Yi (1994) derived self-similar ADAF solutions showing that when the accretion rate ṁ << α2 ṁEdd (where α is the viscosity parameter), the ion temperature approaches the virial value Ti ~ 1012 K while the electron temperature remains much lower (Te ~ 109–1011 K) due to inefficient Coulomb coupling [1]. This two-temperature structure is fundamental: ions carry most of the thermal energy but radiate inefficiently, while electrons produce the observed emission primarily through synchrotron, bremsstrahlung, and inverse Compton processes [1][2].

    Narayan & Yi (1995) extended the theory to include a Bernoulli parameter analysis, showing that ADAF solutions naturally have positive Bernoulli parameters, implying that outflows and winds are energetically favored [2]. This prediction was confirmed by subsequent MHD simulations and is now considered an integral feature of hot accretion flows [3][5].

    Yuan & Narayan (2014) provided a comprehensive review consolidating two decades of theoretical and observational progress on hot accretion flows, establishing the modern framework that includes winds, jets, convection, and magnetic field effects [3].

    Comparison with Standard Thin Disk

    The Shakura & Sunyaev (1973) standard thin disk model applies when the accretion rate is a significant fraction of the Eddington rate [6]. In contrast to RIAFs [1][3]:

    • Geometry: Thin disks are geometrically thin (H/R << 1) and optically thick; RIAFs are geometrically thick (H/R ~ 1) and optically thin [1]
    • Radiative efficiency: Thin disks radiate ~10% of the rest-mass energy; RIAFs radiate orders of magnitude less, with most energy advected or ejected [1][3]
    • Temperature: Thin disks have T ~ 105–107 K with single-temperature gas; RIAFs are two-temperature with Ti >> Te [1]
    • Spectral signatures: Thin disks produce a "big blue bump" thermal UV/optical spectrum; RIAFs produce hard X-ray spectra with weak thermal components [3]
    • Transition: The critical accretion rate separating regimes is ṁcrit ~ α2 ṁEdd ~ 10−2 ṁEdd for typical α ~ 0.1 [3]

    Application to Low-Luminosity AGN

    Most supermassive black holes in the local universe accrete far below the Eddington rate and are classified as low-luminosity AGN (LLAGN) [3]. RIAF models successfully explain the spectral energy distributions of the prototypical LLAGN Sgr A* (the Galactic Center black hole) and M87 [3][7]. The Event Horizon Telescope imaging of M87* and Sgr A* provided direct constraints on the accretion flow geometry, confirming expectations from RIAF theory [8].

    NGC 1275 (3C 84), the central galaxy of the Perseus cluster, hosts a LLAGN with a powerful radio jet [4]. Its accretion flow is modeled as a RIAF, with Bondi accretion from the hot cluster atmosphere feeding the hot flow at large radii [4][9].

    Role as Faraday Screen

    The hot, magnetized gas in a RIAF provides a dense Faraday-rotating medium surrounding the jet base [4][10]. Park et al. (2026) measured Faraday rotation measures (RM) in 3C 84 using VLBI polarimetry at 43–141 GHz and found RM gradients consistent with the jet passing through a magnetized accretion flow [4]. The observed RM values (~104–105 rad m−2) constrain the electron density and magnetic field strength in the circumnuclear medium [4].

    In M87, RIAF models predict RM values of ~105 rad m−2 near the jet base, consistent with EHT polarimetric measurements [8]. The RIAF thus serves as both the fuel reservoir for jet launching and a diagnostic screen whose Faraday rotation properties encode the accretion flow structure [3][4].

    Winds, Jets, and Feedback

    RIAFs naturally produce outflows: the positive Bernoulli parameter of the hot gas drives thermal winds from the accretion flow surface [2][5]. MHD simulations show that these winds can carry away a substantial fraction of the inflowing mass, reducing the actual accretion rate reaching the black hole to ṁ(r) ∝ rs with s ~ 0.5–1 [5][3].

    Magnetically arrested disk (MAD) states in RIAFs efficiently launch relativistic jets via the Blandford-Znajek mechanism [11][12]. The jet power can exceed the radiative luminosity by orders of magnitude in LLAGN, making mechanical AGN feedback dominant over radiative feedback in these systems [3]. In the Perseus cluster, the mechanical power of the 3C 84 jets inflates X-ray cavities and drives weak shocks that heat the intracluster medium [9].

    GRMHD Simulations

    General-relativistic MHD simulations have become the primary tool for studying RIAF dynamics beyond the self-similar approximation [5][12]. Tchekhovskoy et al. (2011) showed that magnetically arrested disks can extract up to ~300% of the accretion power through the Blandford-Znajek mechanism for rapidly spinning black holes [12]. These simulations confirm the two-temperature structure, wind production, and jet launching predicted by analytic RIAF theory [3][5].

    See Also

    • Bondi Accretion — Spherical accretion feeding the RIAF at large radii
    • Faraday Rotation — Diagnostic of the magnetized RIAF plasma
    • 3C 84 / NGC 1275 Jet — LLAGN jet embedded in a RIAF Faraday screen
    • VLBI Polarimetry — Technique probing RIAF structure via rotation measures
    • AGN Feedback — Mechanical feedback dominated by jet power in RIAF systems
    • Perseus Cluster — Prototype system for RIAF-powered jet feedback
    • Park et al. (2026) — RM measurements constraining the 3C 84 accretion flow

    References
    1. Narayan, R. & Yi, I. (1994). "Advection-dominated accretion: A self-similar solution." ApJ, 428, L13. DOI
    2. Narayan, R. & Yi, I. (1995). "Advection-dominated accretion: Self-similarity and bipolar outflows." ApJ, 444, 231. DOI
    3. Yuan, F. & Narayan, R. (2014). "Hot accretion flows around black holes." ARA&A, 52, 529. DOI
    4. Park, J. et al. (2026). "Faraday Rotation Measure of 3C 84." arXiv:2603.16796
    5. Yuan, F., Bu, D., & Wu, M. (2012). "Numerical simulation of hot accretion flows. II. Nature, origin, and properties of outflows." ApJ, 761, 130. DOI
    6. Shakura, N. I. & Sunyaev, R. A. (1973). "Black holes in binary systems. Observational appearance." A&A, 24, 337.
    7. Di Matteo, T., Allen, S. W., Fabian, A. C., Wilson, A. S., & Young, A. J. (2003). "Accretion onto the supermassive black hole in M87." ApJ, 582, 133. DOI
    8. Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration (2021). "First M87 Event Horizon Telescope results. VIII. Magnetic field structure near The Event Horizon." ApJ, 910, L13. DOI
    9. Fabian, A. C. (2012). "Observational evidence of AGN feedback." ARA&A, 50, 455. DOI
    10. Kuo, C. Y. et al. (2014). "Measuring mass accretion rate onto the supermassive black hole in M87 using Faraday rotation measure." ApJ, 783, L33. DOI
    11. Blandford, R. D. & Znajek, R. L. (1977). "Electromagnetic extraction of energy from Kerr black holes." MNRAS, 179, 433. DOI
    12. Tchekhovskoy, A., Narayan, R., & McKinney, J. C. (2011). "Efficient generation of jets from magnetically arrested accretion on rapidly spinning black holes." MNRAS, 418, L79. DOI
    Tags: Accretion · RIAF · ADAF · Hot Accretion Flow · Low-Luminosity AGN · Faraday Rotation · M87 · 3C 84 · Jets
